name: cuDF GPU DataFrames
description: GPU-accelerated DataFrame library for data manipulation and transformation
key_facts: cuDF is a GPU-accelerated DataFrame library that provides a pandas-like API for data manipulation
cuDF uses Apache Arrow columnar format in GPU memory for efficient data access patterns
Common operations like groupby, merge, and sort are parallelized across thousands of GPU cores
cudf.pandas is a zero-code-change accelerator that automatically runs pandas operations on the GPU when possible
String operations in cuDF use the nvStrings library for GPU-accelerated text processing
common_misconceptions: cuDF is a drop-in replacement for pandas in all cases (some pandas operations are not yet supported or behave slightly differently)
Small datasets always benefit from GPU acceleration (GPU overhead makes cuDF slower than pandas for very small datasets)

name: cuML GPU Machine Learning
description: GPU-accelerated machine learning algorithms compatible with scikit-learn API
key_facts: cuML provides GPU-accelerated implementations of common ML algorithms with a scikit-learn-compatible API
Supported algorithms include linear regression, random forests, K-means, DBSCAN, PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP
cuML can be 10x-100x faster than scikit-learn for large datasets due to GPU parallelism
Model objects from cuML can be converted to scikit-learn format for deployment compatibility
cuML integrates with cuDF DataFrames for zero-copy data transfer between data processing and model training
common_misconceptions: cuML supports every scikit-learn algorithm (some algorithms are not yet implemented in cuML)
GPU ML algorithms always produce identical results to CPU versions (floating-point differences can cause minor variations)

name: RAPIDS Ecosystem Integration
description: Integrating RAPIDS with the broader Python data science ecosystem
key_facts: RAPIDS integrates with XGBoost and LightGBM for GPU-accelerated gradient boosting
cuPy provides GPU-accelerated NumPy-compatible array operations used by the RAPIDS ecosystem
RAPIDS can interoperate with PyTorch and TensorFlow via the __cuda_array_interface__ for zero-copy GPU data sharing
The RAPIDS Memory Manager (RMM) provides configurable GPU memory allocation strategies
Jupyter notebooks with RAPIDS enable interactive GPU-accelerated data exploration
common_misconceptions: RAPIDS replaces all CPU-based data science tools (it accelerates compute-intensive operations while CPU tools handle the rest)
RAPIDS requires rewriting entire data pipelines (many operations work with minimal code changes using cudf.pandas)

name: GPU-Accelerated ETL
description: GPU-accelerated extract, transform, and load operations for data science pipelines
key_facts: GPU-accelerated ETL can process terabyte-scale datasets significantly faster than CPU-based approaches
cuDF supports reading Parquet, CSV, JSON, ORC, and Avro file formats directly into GPU memory
GPU-accelerated joins and aggregations parallelize hash-based operations across GPU cores
Data type conversions and null handling in cuDF follow pandas conventions for compatibility
GPU memory limits require chunked processing or distributed approaches for very large datasets
common_misconceptions: All ETL operations benefit equally from GPU acceleration (I/O-bound operations like file reading may not see significant speedup)
GPU ETL eliminates the need for data optimization (efficient file formats and partitioning still matter for performance)

name: GPU-Accelerated Feature Engineering
description: GPU-accelerated feature engineering and preprocessing for machine learning
key_facts: NVTabular provides GPU-accelerated feature engineering for tabular data in recommender systems
GPU-accelerated one-hot encoding, label encoding, and target encoding process categorical features in parallel
cuML provides GPU-accelerated PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP for dimensionality reduction
Feature crosses and polynomial features can be computed efficiently using GPU matrix operations
GPU-accelerated string hashing enables feature hashing for high-cardinality categorical variables
common_misconceptions: Feature engineering on GPUs requires specialized knowledge (RAPIDS APIs mirror familiar pandas and scikit-learn patterns)
Dimensionality reduction is always lossy (techniques like PCA can capture most variance in fewer dimensions with minimal information loss)

name: cuGraph GPU Graph Analytics
description: GPU-accelerated graph analytics library for network analysis and graph algorithms
key_facts: cuGraph provides GPU-accelerated graph algorithms including PageRank, BFS, SSSP, community detection, and triangle counting
Graph data is represented using CSR (Compressed Sparse Row) format for efficient GPU memory access
cuGraph supports both directed and undirected graphs with optional edge weights
GPU acceleration enables interactive graph analytics on graphs with billions of edges
cuGraph integrates with NetworkX providing a GPU-accelerated backend for existing NetworkX code
common_misconceptions: Graph analytics always requires specialized hardware (GPUs significantly accelerate graph algorithms through massive parallelism)
cuGraph can only handle small graphs (it can process graphs with billions of edges using multi-GPU and multi-node setups)

name: Dask-cuDF Distributed GPU Computing
description: Distributed GPU computing using Dask with RAPIDS for multi-GPU and multi-node workflows
key_facts: Dask-cuDF distributes cuDF operations across multiple GPUs and nodes for datasets exceeding single-GPU memory
Dask's lazy evaluation builds a task graph that is optimized and executed only when results are needed
UCX (Unified Communication X) provides high-speed inter-GPU communication for distributed RAPIDS operations
dask-cuda provides GPU-aware worker management including memory limits and device assignment
Partitioning strategies affect load balancing and communication overhead in distributed GPU workflows
common_misconceptions: Dask-cuDF automatically uses all available GPUs (workers must be explicitly configured with GPU assignments)
Distributed processing always improves performance (communication overhead can outweigh benefits for small datasets)

name: Spark RAPIDS Accelerator
description: GPU acceleration for Apache Spark using the RAPIDS Accelerator plugin
key_facts: The RAPIDS Accelerator for Apache Spark runs existing Spark SQL and DataFrame operations on GPUs without code changes
GPU-accelerated Spark operations include joins, aggregations, sorts, and window functions
The qualification tool analyzes existing Spark workloads to estimate GPU acceleration potential
GPU memory spillover to host memory enables processing of datasets larger than GPU memory in Spark
The accelerator plugin works with Spark on YARN, Kubernetes, and standalone cluster managers
common_misconceptions: All Spark operations can be GPU-accelerated (some operations fall back to CPU execution automatically)
RAPIDS Spark requires a completely different cluster setup (it runs on standard Spark clusters with GPU nodes added)

name: Model Deployment with Triton FIL
description: Deploying tree-based and traditional ML models using Triton Inference Server's Forest Inference Library
key_facts: Triton FIL (Forest Inference Library) backend provides GPU-accelerated inference for tree-based models
XGBoost, LightGBM, and scikit-learn random forest models can be served through Triton FIL
GPU-accelerated inference for tree models can be 10-100x faster than CPU inference for batch predictions
Triton supports model ensembles that chain preprocessing, model inference, and postprocessing steps
Model versioning in Triton enables A/B testing and gradual model rollouts
common_misconceptions: Triton only serves deep learning models (it supports tree-based models, classical ML, and custom backends)
Tree-based models do not benefit from GPU acceleration (GPU parallelism significantly speeds up tree traversal for large ensembles)

name: GPU-Accelerated MLOps Pipelines
description: Building end-to-end MLOps pipelines with GPU acceleration from data processing to deployment
key_facts: End-to-end GPU acceleration eliminates data transfer overhead between CPU and GPU processing stages
RAPIDS integrates with MLflow for experiment tracking, model registry, and deployment management
Automated retraining pipelines detect data drift and trigger GPU-accelerated model updates
Feature stores backed by GPU-accelerated computation provide low-latency feature serving for inference
Containerized RAPIDS environments ensure reproducibility across development, testing, and production
common_misconceptions: MLOps for GPU workloads is fundamentally different from CPU-based MLOps (the principles are the same; GPU adds acceleration at each stage)
Feature stores are only for deep learning (tree-based and classical ML models also benefit from centralized feature management)
